course.title: Learn Python
course.description: Master Python from scratch with hands-on interactive coding lessons. From variables to machine learning.

chapter.title: 5. Data Structures
chapter.description: Store, organize, and manipulate collections of data using Lists, Tuples, Dictionaries, and Sets.

lessons.title: Lists
objectives: Add, remove, and modify list items
lessons.explanation: A List is a built-in data structure that allows you to store multiple items in a single variable. Lists are ordered, changeable, and allow duplicate values. They are defined using square brackets `[]`. You can access items in a list by their index (starting at 0).
lessons.analogy: A list is like a row of lockers. Each locker has a number (index) and contains an item. You can open any locker to see what's inside, put new things in, or take things out.
common_mistakes: Forgetting that list indexes start at 0, not 1.
Trying to access an index that doesn't exist (IndexError).
best_practices: Use lists when you have an ordered collection of items of the same type.
lessons.summary: Lists are versatile arrays that can hold any type of data, making them one of the most used data structures in Python.

examples.title: Working with Lists
examples.explanation: We create a list, add 'cherry' to the end using `.append()`, and then print the first item ('apple') and the whole list.

exercises.statement: Create a list named `colors` containing 'red', 'blue', and 'green'. Print the second item in the list.
exercises.starter_code: # Create your list and print the second item
exercises.hint: Remember that indexes start at 0. The second item is at index 1.

lessons.title: Dictionaries
lessons.explanation: Dictionaries are used to store data values in key-value pairs. They are unordered, changeable, and do not allow duplicate keys. Dictionaries are written with curly brackets `{}`, and have keys and values separated by a colon.
lessons.analogy: A dictionary is like a real-world dictionary or a phone book. You look up a specific word or name (the key) to find its definition or phone number (the value).
common_mistakes: Using a mutable object (like a list) as a dictionary key (keys must be immutable, like strings or numbers).
Trying to access a key that doesn't exist (KeyError).
best_practices: Use dictionaries when you need to associate values with unique identifiers (keys).
lessons.summary: Dictionaries provide incredibly fast lookups and are perfect for storing structured data properties.

examples.title: Using Dictionaries
examples.explanation: We access the 'username' key, and then add a brand new key-value pair ('active': True) to the dictionary.

exercises.statement: Create a dictionary named `car` with keys 'brand' (value 'Ford') and 'year' (value 1964). Print the value of the 'year' key.
exercises.hint: Use curly braces {} and separate keys and values with colons :
